content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people numbered from 111 to NNN such that:


**Exactly one** of these people is a thief and always lies;
All the others are honest and always tell the truth.
If the ithi^{th}ith person claims that the thief is **one** person out of Li,Li+1,Li+2,⋯ ,RiL_i, L_i+1, L_i+2, \cdots, R_iLi​,Li​+1,Li​+2,⋯,Ri​, determine how many people could be the thief.

It is guaranteed that at least one person can be the thief.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case will contain NNN, the number of people. NNN lines follow.
The ithi^{th}ith line contains two space-separated integers - LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​, the range of people amongst which the ithi^{th}ith person claims the thief is.


Output Format

For each test case, in the first line, output KKK, the number of possible thieves.
In the next KKK lines, output the list of people that could be the thieves, in **ascending order**.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N, followed by N lines, each containing two space-separated integers Li and Ri.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of possible thieves on the first line, followed by the list of possible thieves in ascending order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Person 1 may be the thief because the other 3 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas he lies and claims that person 2 is the thief. Person 2 may be the thief because the other 3 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas he lies and claims that person 1 is the thief.
Person 1 may be the thief because the other 2 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas he lies and claims that person 1 is the thief.
Person 1 may be the thief because the other 4 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas he lies and claims that person 1 is the thief.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by iterating over each person and checking if they could be the thief. A person can be the thief if they claim the thief is amongst a range of people that includes themselves, and all other people claim the thief is amongst a range of people that includes the person in question.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) per test case, O(T * N^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the possible thieves.
